name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
إِلَـٰهُكُمْ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۚ فَٱلَّذِينَ لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ قُلُوبُهُم مُّنكِرَةٌ وَهُم مُّسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٢٢)
```

```english
Your God is the One God. Those who do not believe in the Hereafter, their hearts are in denial [of the truth], and they are arrogant. (22)
```

```arabic
لَا جَرَمَ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَعْلَمُ مَا يُسِرُّونَ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُسْتَكْبِرِينَ (٢٣)
```

```english
Undoubtedly, Allah knows whatever they hide and whatever they disclose. Indeed, He does not like the arrogant. (23)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا قِيلَ لَهُم مَّاذَآ أَنزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ ۙ قَالُوٓا۟ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٢٤)
```

```english
When they are asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’ They say, ‘Myths of the ancients,’ (24)
```

```arabic
لِيَحْمِلُوٓا۟ أَوْزَارَهُمْ كَامِلَةً يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ ۙ وَمِنْ أَوْزَارِ ٱلَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُم بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ ۗ أَلَا سَآءَ مَا يَزِرُونَ (٢٥)
```

```english
[with the result] that they will bear the full weight of their own burden on the Day of Resurrection, along with part of the burden of those whom they mislead without any knowledge. Behold! Evil is what they bear! (25)
```

```arabic
قَدْ مَكَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَى ٱللَّهُ بُنْيَـٰنَهُم مِّنَ ٱلْقَوَاعِدِ فَخَرَّ عَلَيْهِمُ ٱلسَّقْفُ مِن فَوْقِهِمْ وَأَتَىٰهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ مِنْ حَيْثُ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٢٦)
```

```english
Those who were before them [had also] schemed. Then Allah came at their edifice from the foundations and the roof fell down upon them from above and the punishment overtook them whence they were not aware. (26)
```

```arabic
ثُمَّ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُخْزِيهِمْ وَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكَآءِىَ ٱلَّذِينَ كُنتُمْ تُشَـٰٓقُّونَ فِيهِمْ ۚ قَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمَ إِنَّ ٱلْخِزْىَ ٱلْيَوْمَ وَٱلسُّوٓءَ عَلَى ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٢٧)
```

```english
Then, on the Day of Resurrection, He will disgrace them, and say, ‘Where are My “partners” for whose sake you used to defy [Allah]?’ Those who were given knowledge will say, ‘Indeed today disgrace and distress pursue the faithless.’ (27)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ ظَالِمِىٓ أَنفُسِهِمْ ۖ فَأَلْقَوُا۟ ٱلسَّلَمَ مَا كُنَّا نَعْمَلُ مِن سُوٓءٍۭ ۚ بَلَىٰٓ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلِيمٌۢ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٢٨)
```

```english
—Those whom the angels take away while they were wronging themselves. Thereat they submit: ‘We were not doing any evil!’ ‘Yes, [the angels say to them,] indeed Allah knows best what you used to do! (28)
```

```arabic
فَٱدْخُلُوٓا۟ أَبْوَٰبَ جَهَنَّمَ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۖ فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى ٱلْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ (٢٩)
```

```english
Enter the gates of hell to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the [final] abode of the arrogant.’ (29)
```

```arabic
۞ وَقِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ مَاذَآ أَنزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ ۚ قَالُوا۟ خَيْرًا ۗ لِّلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا۟ فِى هَـٰذِهِ ٱلدُّنْيَا حَسَنَةٌ ۚ وَلَدَارُ ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ ۚ وَلَنِعْمَ دَارُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣٠)
```

```english
But those who were Godwary will be asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’ They will say, ‘Good.’ For those who do good in this world there will be a good [reward], and the abode of the Hereafter is better, and the abode of the Godwary is surely excellent: (30)
```

```arabic
جَنَّـٰتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَهَا تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ ۖ لَهُمْ فِيهَا مَا يَشَآءُونَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يَجْزِى ٱللَّهُ ٱلْمُتَّقِينَ (٣١)
```

```english
the Gardens of Eden, which they will enter, with streams running in them. There they will have whatever they wish, and thus does Allah reward the Godwary (31)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّىٰهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ ۙ يَقُولُونَ سَلَـٰمٌ عَلَيْكُمُ ٱدْخُلُوا۟ ٱلْجَنَّةَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٣٢)
```

```english
—those whom the angels take away while they are pure. They say [to them], ‘Peace be to you! Enter paradise because of what you used to do.’ (32)
```

```arabic
هَلْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَّآ أَن تَأْتِيَهُمُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِىَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ وَمَا ظَلَمَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَلَـٰكِن كَانُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ (٣٣)
```

```english
Do they not consider that the angels may come to them, or your Lord’s edict may come? Those who were before them had acted likewise; Allah did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves. (33)
```

```arabic
فَأَصَابَهُمْ سَيِّـَٔاتُ مَا عَمِلُوا۟ وَحَاقَ بِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (٣٤)
```

```english
So the evils of what they had earned visited them, and they were besieged by what they used to deride. (34)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ لَوْ شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ مَا عَبَدْنَا مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ نَّحْنُ وَلَآ ءَابَآؤُنَا وَلَا حَرَّمْنَا مِن دُونِهِۦ مِن شَىْءٍ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ فَعَلَ ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ فَهَلْ عَلَى ٱلرُّسُلِ إِلَّا ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (٣٥)
```

```english
The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshiped anything besides Him—neither we, nor our fathers—nor we would have forbidden anything without His sanction.’ Those who were before them had acted likewise. Is the apostles’ duty anything but to communicate in clear terms? (35)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ بَعَثْنَا فِى كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَّسُولًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ ٱلطَّـٰغُوتَ ۖ فَمِنْهُم مَّنْ هَدَى ٱللَّهُ وَمِنْهُم مَّنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ ٱلضَّلَـٰلَةُ ۚ فَسِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُكَذِّبِينَ (٣٦)
```

```english
Certainly We raised an apostle in every nation [to preach:] ‘Worship Allah, and shun fake deities.’ Among them were some whom Allah guided, and among them were some who deserved to be in error. So travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the deniers. (36)
```

```arabic
إِن تَحْرِصْ عَلَىٰ هُدَىٰهُمْ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَهْدِى مَن يُضِلُّ ۖ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (٣٧)
```

```english
Even if you are eager for them to be guided, indeed Allah does not guide those who mislead [others], and they will have no helpers. (37)
```

```arabic
وَأَقْسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمَـٰنِهِمْ ۙ لَا يَبْعَثُ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَمُوتُ ۚ بَلَىٰ وَعْدًا عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٣٨)
```

```english
They swear by Allah with solemn oaths that Allah will not resurrect those who die. Yes indeed [He will], it is a promise binding upon Him, but most people do not know, (38)
```

```arabic
لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمُ ٱلَّذِى يَخْتَلِفُونَ فِيهِ وَلِيَعْلَمَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ كَـٰذِبِينَ (٣٩)
```

```english
so that He may clarify for them what they differ about, and that the faithless may know that they were liars. (39)
```

```arabic
إِنَّمَا قَوْلُنَا لِشَىْءٍ إِذَآ أَرَدْنَـٰهُ أَن نَّقُولَ لَهُۥ كُن فَيَكُونُ (٤٠)
```

```english
All that We say to a thing, when We will it, is to say to it ‘Be!’ and it is. (40)
```

## (Commentary)

This is the second part of the verses from the opening of the Surah. The first part encompassed the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His governance and sustenance of the universe* — and the establishment of proof against the polytheists regarding it, after He warned them of the coming of the Command and declared Allah, the Glorified, transcendent above their polytheism (شركهم).

This second part includes what is appropriate to mention in this context regarding the evil traits of the polytheists that branch out from their denial of monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — and their false claims, such as their arrogance toward Allah, their mockery of His signs, and their denial of the Resurrection (الحشر). It also clarifies the invalidity of these claims, exposes their corruption, threatens them with the coming of the Command and the descent of worldly punishment, and warns of the punishment on the day of death and the Day of Resurrection, along with other realities that will be uncovered through investigation.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَالَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ قُلُوبُهُمْ مُنْكِرَةٌ وَهُمْ مُسْتَكْبِرُونَ** — "Your God is the One God. But as for those who do not believe in the Hereafter, their hearts are resistant and they are arrogant." (16:22). The discussion regarding His saying: **إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ** ("Your God is the One God") has already preceded, noting that it is the conclusion of the proof established in the previous verses.

His saying: **فَالَّذِينَ لا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِالْآخِرَةِ** ("But as for those who do not believe in the Hereafter") etc., is a derivation from it and the opening of a new section of discourse concerning the deeds of the disbelievers (الكفار) — both their words and actions — arising from their lack of faith in Allah, the Glorified. He specifically mentioned their lack of faith in the Hereafter (الآخرة) rather than their lack of faith in Allah alone because the matter for which the proof was established is complete monotheism (التوحيد). This is the necessity of believing in a God who is All-Knowing and All-Powerful, Who created everything and perfected His blessing, not in vain or falsehood, but with truth (بالحق), so that they may return to Him, and He may call them to account for what they did and requite them for what they earned of the commands and prohibitions He entrusted to them through the messengers.

Thus, the monotheism (التوحيد) called for in the preceding verses is the affirmation of His oneness, the Exalted, faith in what the messengers of Allah brought, and faith in the Day of Reckoning and Requital. For this reason, He described the disbelievers as not believing in the Hereafter, because faith in it necessitates faith in Oneness (الوحدانية) and the Message (الرسالة).

To further clarify what we have mentioned, you may refer to His saying at the beginning of the verses: **يُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ بِالرُّوحِ مِنْ أَمْرِهِ عَلى مَنْ يَشاءُ مِنْ عِبادِهِ أَنْ أَنْذِرُوا أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا أَنَا فَاتَّقُونِ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ بِالْحَقِّ تَعالى عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — "He sends down the angels with the Spirit of His command to whomever He wishes of His servants: ‘Warn [people] that there is no god except Me; so be wary of Me.’ He created the heavens and the earth with consummate wisdom. He is far above having any partners they ascribe [to Him]." (16:2-3). This is a comprehensive discourse covering the three fundamental principles (الأصول الثلاثة).

His saying: **قُلُوبُهُمْ مُنْكِرَةٌ** ("their hearts are resistant") means they deny the truth. His saying: **وَهُمْ مُسْتَكْبِرُونَ** ("and they are arrogant") means they are too proud to accept the truth. Arrogance (الاستكبار) — according to what scholars have mentioned — is the seeking of superiority by refusing to submit to the truth.

The meaning is: Your God is One, as indicated by the verses that are clear in their significance. Since the matter is of such clarity and manifestness that it cannot be hidden by any veil nor doubted by any intellect, those who do not believe in the Hereafter have hearts that deny the truth and reject it out of stubbornness, and they are too arrogant to submit to the truth without any proof or evidence.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لا جَرَمَ أَنَّ اللهَ يَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ إِنَّهُ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُسْتَكْبِرِينَ** — "Undoubtedly Allah knows what they keep secret and what they disclose. Indeed He does not love the arrogant." (16:23). **لا جَرَمَ** ("Undoubtedly") is a compound phrase that remains in a single state, conveying the meaning of certainty (التحقيق), as mentioned by al-Khalil and Sibawayh. What others have mentioned returns to this, even if they differ on the origin of its composition. Al-Khalil said: "It is a word of certainty and only occurs as a response; it is said, 'They did such and such,' and the listener says, 'Undoubtedly (لا جرم) they will regret it.'"

The meaning is: It is certain — or it is a fact — **أَنَّ اللهَ يَعْلَمُ ما يُسِرُّونَ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ** ("that Allah knows what they keep secret and what they disclose"). This is an allusion and a threat of an evil requital; that is, He knows what they hide of their deeds and what they manifest, so He will requite them for what they did and hold them accountable for what they denied and their arrogance. Indeed, He does not love the arrogant.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** — "When they are asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’ they say, ‘Myths of the ancients!’" (16:24). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The word *satr* (السطر) — with a *fathah* then *sukun*, or with two *fathahs* — refers to a line of writing, a row of planted trees, or a row of standing people..." until he said, "...and the plural of *satr* is *astur* (أسطر), *sutur* (سطور), and *astar* (أسطار)."

He said: "As for His saying: **أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** ('Myths of the ancients'), al-Mubarrad said: It is the plural of *usturah* (أسطورة), similar to *urjuhah* and *arajih* (swings), *uthfiyah* and *athafi* (tripod stones), and *uhduthah* and *ahadith* (tales)." And His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** means: something they wrote as a lie and falsehood according to their claim, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ اكْتَتَبَها فَهِيَ تُمْلى عَلَيْهِ بُكْرَةً وَأَصِيلاً** — "‘Myths of the ancients,’ they say, ‘which he has got written down, and they are dictated to him morning and evening.’" (25:5). [End of quote]. Others said: *Asatir* (أساطير) is the plural of *astar* (أسطار), and *astar* is the plural of *satr* (سطر); thus, it is a plural of a plural.

Regarding His saying: **وَإِذا قِيلَ لَهُمْ ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ** ("When they are asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’"), it is possible that the questioner was one of the believers, saying it to test their state and inquire about their view of the prophetic call. It is also possible that the questioner was one of the polytheists, saying it to them so he might follow them in their view; he expressed the Quran with a phrase like **ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ** ("What is it that your Lord has sent down?") as a form of sarcasm and mockery. It is also possible that the questioner was a doubter, confused and searching. The following verse, as well as His saying later: **وَقِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ** ("But those who were God-conscious are asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’" [16:30]), supports one of the latter two possibilities.

Their saying: **قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** ("they say, ‘Myths of the ancients!’") means that what is being asked about are superstitious lies that the ancients wrote, established, and left for those after them. This statement necessitates the claim that it was not sent down from Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** — "So that they may bear their full burdens on the Day of Resurrection..." (16:25) to the end of the verse. It is said in *al-Mufradat*: "*al-Wazar* (الوزر) — with two *fathahs* — is a refuge in a mountain to which one retreats. The Exalted said: **كَلَّا لا وَزَرَ إِلى رَبِّكَ يَوْمَئِذٍ الْمُسْتَقَرُّ** — 'No indeed! There will be no refuge! With your Lord shall be the [final] resting place that day.' (75:11-12). And *al-wizr* (الوزر) — with a *kasrah* then *sukun* — is a heavy weight, by way of comparison to the weight of a mountain. It is used to express sin (الإثم) just as it is expressed as a burden (الثقل). The Exalted said: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً** ('So that they may bear their full burdens'), which is like His saying: **وَلَيَحْمِلُنَّ أَثْقالَهُمْ وَأَثْقالاً مَعَ أَثْقالِهِمْ** — 'They will surely carry their own burdens, and [other] burdens along with their own burdens.'" (29:13).

He said: "Bearing the burden (وزر) of another is, in reality, in the manner alluded to by the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in his saying: **من سن سنة حسنة كان له أجرها ـ وأجر من عمل بها من غير أن ينقص من أجره شيء ، ومن سن سنة سيئة كان له وزرها ووزر من عمل بها** — 'Whoever establishes a good tradition (سنة) will have its reward and the reward of whoever acts upon it without their reward being diminished in the least; and whoever establishes an evil tradition will have its burden (وزر) and the burden of whoever acts upon it,' meaning the like of the burden of the one who acts upon it. And His saying: **وَلا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** ('and no bearer shall bear another’s burden' [6:164]) means: one does not bear the burden of another such that the original bearer is divested of it." [End of quote].

What he mentioned of the prophetic tradition (الحديث النبوي) is narrated through both the Shi'a (الخاصة) and Sunni (العامة) chains of transmission, and it is confirmed by the Mighty Book in verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَاتَّبَعَتْهُمْ ذُرِّيَّتُهُمْ بِإِيمانٍ أَلْحَقْنا بِهِمْ ذُرِّيَّتَهُمْ وَما أَلَتْناهُمْ مِنْ عَمَلِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ كُلُّ امْرِئٍ بِما كَسَبَ رَهِينٌ** — "As for those who believe and whose descendants follow them in faith, We will unite their descendants with them, and We will not stint anything of their [own] deeds. Every man is a hostage to what he has earned." (52:21), and His saying: **وَنَكْتُبُ ما قَدَّمُوا وَآثارَهُمْ** — "and We write down what they have sent ahead and their effects [which they left behind]." (36:12). The verses with this meaning are numerous.

As for his interpretation of the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "he will have its burden and the burden of whoever acts upon it," meaning *the like of* the burden of the one who acts upon it, this is an outward explanation (كلام ظاهري). There is no harm in using it to interpret the verse and the narration to resolve the apparent contradiction between them and verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **لا تَزِرُ وازِرَةٌ وِزْرَ أُخْرى** — "and no bearer shall bear another’s burden" (6:164) and His saying: **لَيُوَفِّيَنَّهُمْ رَبُّكَ أَعْمالَهُمْ** — "your Lord will surely pay them in full for their deeds" (11:111). For if the one who commands bore the burden of the evil deed and was punished for it while the doer was not, it would contradict the first verse. If the burden were divided between them, each bearing a portion and receiving a portion of the punishment, it would contradict the second verse. However, if the one who establishes the tradition or commands it bears the *like* of what the doer bears, it contradicts nothing.

In reality, just as the deed is a single act — whether good or evil — so too its burden and punishment are, for instance, one, without multiplicity. However, since the deed itself is sustained by more than one person — its relation to the commander and the doer being a vertical (طوليا) relation, not a horizontal (عرضيا) one that would necessitate an impossibility — its consequence of burden and punishment is sustained by more than one. Thus, there is a single burden borne by two, and a single punishment by which both the commander and the doer are punished.

This is easily conceptualized by reflecting on the content of the verses based on the embodiment of deeds (تجسم الأعمال). For a deed, such as an evil one, assuming its embodiment, is a single individual entity that manifests for two and punishes two people by its manifestation: the commander and the doer, or the one who establishes the tradition and the one who follows it. It is, in a remote sense, like a single person being perceived by two people, both of whom feel pleasure or pain because of him, while he remains but one.

Some discussion on this meaning has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **لِيَمِيزَ اللهُ الْخَبِيثَ مِنَ الطَّيِّبِ** ("so that Allah may separate the bad from the good" [8:37]) in the ninth volume of this book, and a detailed discourse on it will follow, Allah willing, in its appropriate place.

In any case, His saying: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ وَمِنْ أَوْزارِ الَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُمْ بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** ("So that they may bear their full burdens on the Day of Resurrection, and some of the burdens of those whom they mislead without any knowledge") — the *lam* (لـ) denotes the ultimate end (للغاية) and is connected to His saying: **قالُوا أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** ("they say, ‘Myths of the ancients!’"). In His saying: **يُضِلُّونَهُمْ** ("they mislead them"), there is an indication that their bearing the burdens of others is only due to their misleading them. Thus, the misleading becomes the end, and the bearing becomes the ultimate end. The implied meaning is: they said "myths of the ancients" to mislead them, while they themselves were astray, so they shall bear their own burdens in full and some of the burdens of those whom they mislead without knowledge.

In qualifying His saying: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ** ("So that they may bear their burdens") with the word **كامِلَةً** ("full"), He dispels the illusion of division or partiality — that they might bear some of their own burdens and some of the burdens of those they mislead, such that the total becomes full burdens. Rather, they bear their own burdens in full, and *then* some of the burdens of those they mislead.

His saying: **وَمِنْ أَوْزارِ الَّذِينَ يُضِلُّونَهُمْ** ("and some of the burdens of those whom they mislead") — the word *min* (مِنْ) denotes partitivity (تبعيضية) because they do not bear *all* of their burdens, but only those burdens that resulted specifically from their misleading them, as evidenced by the context. Thus, the partitivity is to distinguish the burdens resulting from misleading from others, not to indicate the division of each individual burden of misleading, bearing part of it here and part of it there, nor the division of the total burdens of misleading, bearing a portion here and a portion there while being divested of the other portion. For verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَنْ يَعْمَلْ مِثْقالَ ذَرَّةٍ شَرًّا يَرَهُ** — "and whoever does an atom’s weight of evil will see it" (99:8) negate that. So understand this.

From what has preceded, the weakness of what some have inferred from His saying: **لِيَحْمِلُوا أَوْزارَهُمْ كامِلَةً يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ** becomes apparent — namely, that it implies nothing was diminished from them nor expiated by any affliction that befell them in the world or any accepted act of obedience therein, as the burdens of the believers are expiated.

Similarly weak is what others inferred: that the verse indicates that He, the Exalted, may waive some punishment for the believers; for if this meaning were applicable to everyone, there would be no benefit in specifying these disbelievers with it.

The reason for this weakness is that while what they mentioned regarding the disgrace of the disbelievers and the honoring of the believers is true in itself — as indicated by verses showing the disgrace of the disbelievers through what befalls them in the world, the nullification of their deeds, and the inclusion of a group of believers in forgiveness and intercession — this verse is not looking at any of that. Rather, the focus here is on the distinction between their own burdens and the burdens of others whom they misled, and that the second category attaches to them in part — specifically those burdens resulting from the misleading — unlike the first category, which belongs to them entirely.

Even weaker than both is what some mentioned: that *min* (مِنْ) in His saying **وَمِنْ أَوْزارِ الَّذِينَ** ("and some of the burdens of those...") is redundant (زائدة) or explanatory (بيانية), which is clearly incorrect.

His qualification, the Glorified, of His saying: **يُضِلُّونَهُمْ** ("they mislead them") with the phrase **بِغَيْرِ عِلْمٍ** ("without any knowledge") is to indicate that those whom these polytheists — who said "myths of the ancients" — misled, only went astray by following them in imitation (تقليدا) and without knowledge. Thus, the speakers are the leaders of misguidance, and those astray are their followers and imitators. Then He, the Glorified, concluded the verse by censuring them and decrying the state of all of them, saying: **أَلا ساءَ ما يَزِرُونَ** — "Behold! Evil is what they bear!" (16:25).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قَدْ مَكَرَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَأَتَى اللهُ بُنْيانَهُمْ مِنَ الْقَواعِدِ** — "Those who were before them [also] plotted. Then Allah came at their edifice from the foundations..." (16:26) etc. His, the Exalted, coming at their edifice from the foundations is the presence of His command where it was not previously present. This is common in speech. The "falling of the roof" (خرور السقف) is its collapse to the ground and its destruction.

It appears — as the context suggests — that His saying: **فأَتَى اللهُ بُنْيانَهُمْ مِنَ الْقَواعِدِ فَخَرَّ عَلَيْهِمُ السَّقْفُ مِنْ فَوْقِهِمْ** ("Then Allah came at their edifice from the foundations, and the roof fell on them from above them") is an allusion to the nullification of their plot and the corruption of their scheme from where they did not expect. It is like one who guards his front and watches it, only for the enemy to come from behind him. Thus, Allah, the Glorified, comes at the edifice of their plot from the side of its foundations while they are watching its roof for what might come from above; so the roof collapses upon them, not by a destroyer demolishing it from above, but by the collapse of the foundations.

Accordingly, His saying: **وَأَتاهُمُ الْعَذابُ مِنْ حيثُ لا يَشْعُرُونَ** ("and the punishment came at them from whence they were not aware") is an explanatory conjunction (عطف تفسيري) that explains His saying: **فَأَتَى اللهُ بُنْيانَهُمْ** ("Then Allah came at their edifice") etc. The intended punishment is the worldly punishment.

In the verse is a threat to the polytheists who were plotting against Allah and His Messenger, by reminding them of what Allah did to the plotters before them among the arrogant of past nations, where He turned their plot back upon themselves, so they were the ones plotted against.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **ثُمَّ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ يُخْزِيهِمْ وَيَقُولُ أَيْنَ شُرَكائِيَ الَّذِينَ كُنْتُمْ تُشَاقُّونَ فِيهِمْ** — "Then on the Day of Resurrection He will disgrace them, and He will say, ‘Where are My partners for whose sake you used to be so contentious?’" (16:27). Disgrace (الإخزاء) comes from *khizy* (خزي), which, as al-Raghib mentioned, is the humiliation that causes shame. Contentiousness (المشاقة) comes from *shaqq* (شق), which is to cut a part of something and separate it. It refers to quarreling, enmity, and disagreement from those whose nature is to be harmonious and united. Thus, the polytheists' contentiousness regarding their partners is their disagreement with the people of monotheism (التوحيد), though they were one nation whom Allah created upon monotheism and the true religion, and their quarreling with them and separation from them.

The meaning is: Allah, the Glorified, will disgrace them on the Day of Resurrection and strike them with humiliation and lowliness by saying: "Where are My partners for whose sake you used to contend with the people of truth, quarrel with them, and create disagreement in the religion of Allah?"

**His saying, the Exalted**: **قالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ إِنَّ الْخِزْيَ الْيَوْمَ وَالسُّوءَ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ** — "Those who were given knowledge will say, ‘Indeed today disgrace and evil have overtaken the faithless’" (16:27). Disgrace (الخزي) is the humiliation of the standing-place, and evil (السوء) is the punishment, as the context implies.

These whom Allah described as having been given knowledge and informed that they speak thus, are those who were granted knowledge of Allah and to whom the reality of monotheism (التوحيد) was uncovered. This is what the context provides by way of contrast between them — being described with knowledge — and the polytheists, to whom it will be uncovered that day that they were only worshipping names they had named and a mirage they had imagined.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified, informs about them that they speak on that day and say such-and-such, while He said in describing that day: **لا يَتَكَلَّمُونَ إِلَّا مَنْ أَذِنَ لَهُ الرَّحْمنُ وَقالَ صَواباً** — "they will not speak, except him whom the All-beneficent has permitted and who says what is right." (78:38). Speech cannot be "right" (صواباً) in the true sense of the meaning unless its speaker is protected from error, nonsense, and falsehood. One cannot be protected in his speech unless he is protected in his action and his knowledge. Thus, these are a people who see only the truth, do only the truth, and speak only the truth.

If you say: "Then those who were given knowledge, based on this interpretation, are the people of infallibility (أهل العصمة); but this is refuted by the frequent occurrence of this phrase in His, the Exalted, speech where others are intended, such as His saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ وَيْلَكُمْ ثَوابُ اللهِ خَيْرٌ** — 'But those who were given knowledge said, "Woe to you! Allah’s reward is better..."' (28:80), and His saying: **وَلِيَعْلَمَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْعِلْمَ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ رَبِّكَ فَيُؤْمِنُوا بِهِ** — 'and so that those who have been given knowledge may know that it is the truth from your Lord, and so believe in it...' (22:54), among other instances where it is apparent that infallibility (العصمة) is not intended by the giving of knowledge."

I say: What we mentioned is an inference drawn from the context of this specific place, not that it is the meaning of the term whenever it is used in His, the Exalted, speech.

As for their saying that those who were given knowledge are the prophets only, or the prophets and the believers who knew the proofs of monotheism in the world, or the believers alone, or the angels — there is no evidence in His, the Exalted, speech for any of these specifically.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ فَأَلْقَوُا السَّلَمَ** — "Those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves. Thereat they will offer submission..." (16:28) to the end of the verse. It appears that this is an explanation of "the faithless" (الكافرين) mentioned at the end of the previous verse, just as His coming saying: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ** ("Those whom the angels take away while they are pure" [16:32]) etc., is an explanation of "the God-conscious" (المتقين) mentioned at the end of the verse before it. Its being an explanation of "the faithless" does not necessitate it being part of the speech of those who were given knowledge, such that the order of the discourse would be disrupted by their saying: **إِنَّ الْخِزْيَ الْيَوْمَ** ("Indeed today disgrace...") etc., then their explanation by saying: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ** ("Those whom the angels take away...") etc., without saying "those whom the angels *took* away," as is not hidden.

His saying: **فَأَلْقَوُا السَّلَمَ** ("Thereat they will offer submission") means surrender, which is humility and compliance. The plural pronoun refers to the disbelievers. The meaning is: the disbelievers are those whom the angels take away and seize their souls while they are wronging themselves through their disbelief in Allah. So they offer submission and present humility and compliance, claiming thereby that they used to do no evil. Their claim is then rejected and they are called liars, and it is said to them: "Yes, indeed you did! Allah knows best what you used to do before you arrived at this place, which is death."

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَادْخُلُوا أَبْوابَ جَهَنَّمَ خالِدِينَ فِيها فَلَبِئْسَ مَثْوَى الْمُتَكَبِّرِينَ** — "‘Enter the gates of hell, to remain in it [forever]. Evil is the abode of the arrogant.’" (16:29). The address is to the collective, just as His saying: **إِنَّ الْخِزْيَ الْيَوْمَ وَالسُّوءَ عَلَى الْكافِرِينَ** ("Indeed today disgrace and evil have overtaken the faithless") and His saying: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ** ("Those whom the angels take away...") etc., were looking at the group of disbelievers rather than each individual separately.

Accordingly, its meaning returns to something like our saying: "Let each one of you enter a gate of Hell that suits his deed and his position of disbelief," not that each one of them enters all the gates or more than one of them. The discussion regarding the meaning of the gates of Hell has already preceded in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **لَها سَبْعَةُ أَبْوابٍ لِكُلِّ بابٍ مِنْهُمْ جُزْءٌ مَقْسُومٌ** — "It has seven gates, and to each gate belongs a separate group of them." (15:44).

The "arrogant" (المتكبرون) are the same as the "proud" (المستكبرون) in terms of their referent, even if the linguistic focus differs between them, like "Muslim" (المسلم) and "one who submits" (المستسلم). The *mustakbir* (المستكبر) is one who seeks greatness for himself by bringing it from potentiality to actuality and manifesting it to others, while the *mutakabbir* (المتكبر) is one who accepts it for himself and takes it as an attribute.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقِيلَ لِلَّذِينَ اتَّقَوْا ما ذا أَنْزَلَ رَبُّكُمْ قالُوا خَيْراً** — "But those who were God-conscious are asked, ‘What is it that your Lord has sent down?’ They say, ‘Good!’" (16:30) to the end of the verse.

Those questioned are identified as those who were God-conscious (اتقوا), meaning those whose state in the world was that they were clothed in godwariness (التقوى) — *the protective shield of piety and mindfulness of Allah* — and they are those characterized by it continuously, as evidenced by the repetition of their mention afterward with the term "the God-conscious" (المتقين) twice. Thus, those questioned from this group are their elite who are perfect in faith, just as those questioned in the other group were their worst who were perfect in disbelief, namely the arrogant.

Therefore, the saying of some — that those who were God-conscious refers to all believers who avoided polytheism, or polytheism and sins in general — is out of place.

Their saying: **قالُوا خَيْراً** ("They say, ‘Good!’") means: He sent down good, because He sent down a Quran containing knowledge and laws, in the adoption and practice of which is the good of this world and the Hereafter. In their saying **خَيْراً** ("Good!") is an admission that the Quran is sent down from Him, the Exalted, in addition to their describing it as good. In this, they manifest their opposition to the arrogant, who answered by saying: **أَساطِيرُ الْأَوَّلِينَ** ("Myths of the ancients!"), meaning "it is myths." If the God-conscious had said *khayrun* (خير) in the nominative case, it would not have contained an admission of its being sent down, just as if the arrogant had said *asatira* (أساطير) in the accusative case, it would have contained an admission of its being sent down. So it has been said.

His saying: **لِلَّذِينَ أَحْسَنُوا فِي هذِهِ الدُّنْيا حَسَنَةٌ وَلَدارُ الْآخِرَةِ خَيْرٌ** — "For those who do good in this world there will be a good reward, and the abode of the Hereafter is better..." (16:30). The apparent context is that this is an explanation of their saying "Good!" Is it the completion of their speech or an explanation from Him, the Exalted? The appearance of His saying: **وَلَنِعْمَ دارُ الْمُتَّقِينَ جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ** ("and the abode of the God-conscious is surely excellent: the Gardens of Eden..." [16:30-31]) to the end of the verse is that it is speech from Him, the Exalted, by which He explains the nature of the "good" in what He sent down to them. For it resembles the speech of the Lord, the Exalted, more than the speech of the servant, especially the God-conscious who would not venture such proposals.

What is meant by "good reward" (الحسنة) is the beautiful recompense. This is because, through "doing good" (الإحسان) — which is acting according to what the Book contains — they are granted a righteous society in which justice and goodness prevail, and a pleasant life built upon guidance and happiness; they attain that as a worldly reward for their goodness because of His saying: "for them in the world." And the abode of the life of the Hereafter is a better reward because in it is permanence without end, blessing without affliction, and happiness with which there is no misery.

The meaning of the verse is: It was said to the God-conscious among the believers, "What has your Lord sent down of the Book, and what is its nature?" They said, "He sent down good." Its being good is that for those who do good — that is, act according to what is in it, so "doing good" was placed in the position of adopting and acting upon what is in the Book as an allusion that what the Book commands are good deeds — there is in this world a beautiful recompense, and the abode of the Hereafter is a better reward for them.

Then He praised their abode to be a confirmation of the statement, saying: **وَلَنِعْمَ دارُ الْمُتَّقِينَ** ("and the abode of the God-conscious is surely excellent"). Then He explained the abode of the God-conscious by His saying: **جَنَّاتُ عَدْنٍ يَدْخُلُونَها تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ لَهُمْ فِيها ما يَشاؤُنَ كَذلِكَ يَجْزِي اللهُ الْمُتَّقِينَ** — "the Gardens of Eden, which they will enter, with rivers running in them. There they will have whatever they wish. Thus does Allah reward the God-conscious." (16:31). The meaning is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ طَيِّبِينَ يَقُولُونَ سَلامٌ عَلَيْكُمْ ادْخُلُوا الْجَنَّةَ بِما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ** — "Those whom the angels take away while they are pure. They say [to them], ‘Peace be to you! Enter paradise because of what you used to do.’" (16:32). This is an explanation of "the God-conscious" (المتقين), just as His saying: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ** ("Those whom the angels take away while they are wronging themselves") etc., was an explanation of "the arrogant" (المستكبرين).

Purity (الطيب) is the stripping of a thing of what mixes with it, thereby clouding it and removing its sincerity and pureness. It is said: "Life became pure (طاب) for me," meaning it became clear and stripped of what clouds or diminishes it. "Pure speech" (القول الطيب) is that which is free from nonsense, insult, harshness, and everything else that causes a flaw in it. The difference between purity (الطيب) and cleanliness (الطهارة) is that cleanliness is a thing being upon its original nature such that it is free from what causes aversion to it, while purity is its being upon its origin without being mixed with what clouds it and corrupts its state, whether one is averse to it or not. For this reason, "pure" (الطيب) is contrasted with "vile" (الخبيث), which contains added impurity. The Exalted said: **الْخَبِيثاتُ لِلْخَبِيثِينَ وَالْخَبِيثُونَ لِلْخَبِيثاتِ وَالطَّيِّباتُ لِلطَّيِّبِينَ وَالطَّيِّبُونَ لِلطَّيِّباتِ** — "Vile women are for vile men, and vile men are for vile women, and good women are for good men, and good men are for good women." (24:26). And He said: **وَالْبَلَدُ الطَّيِّبُ يَخْرُجُ نَباتُهُ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِ وَالَّذِي خَبُثَ لا يَخْرُجُ إِلَّا نَكِداً** — "As for the good land, its vegetation comes out by the leave of its Lord; and as for that which is bad, it does not yield except scantily." (7:58).

Accordingly, what is meant by the God-conscious being "pure" (طيبين) at the time of their being taken away is their sincerity from the impurity of wrongdoing (الظلم), in contrast to the arrogant whom He described as "wronging themselves" at the time of being taken away in the previous verse: **الَّذِينَ تَتَوَفَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ ظالِمِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ**. The meaning of the verse is that the God-conscious are those whom the angels take away while they are stripped of the impurity of wrongdoing — polytheism and sins. They say to them, "Peace be to you" — which is a verbal granting of security to them — "Enter paradise because of what you used to do" — which is a guidance for them toward it.

The verse — as you see — describes the God-conscious as being free from being clothed in wrongdoing and promises them security and guidance to Paradise. Thus, its content returns to the meaning of His saying: **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَلَمْ يَلْبِسُوا إِيمانَهُمْ بِظُلْمٍ أُولئِكَ لَهُمُ الْأَمْنُ وَهُمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** — "Those who have faith and do not taint their faith with wrongdoing—for such there shall be safety, and they are the [rightly] guided." (6:82).

Some commentators mentioned that what is meant by "pure" in the verse is cleanliness from the filth of polytheism, and some interpreted it as their words and deeds being wholesome. Most interpret it as cleanliness from the foulness of sins. By reflecting on what has preceded, you will know that none of what they mentioned is free from some imprecision.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** — "Do they look for anything but that the angels should come to them, or that your Lord’s edict should come? Those who were before them acted likewise..." (16:33) etc. This is a return to the account of the arrogant among the polytheists, mentioning some of their states and sayings, and comparing them to those who preceded them among the tyrants of past nations and what their matter resulted in.

Regarding His saying: **هَلْ يَنْظُرُونَ إِلَّا أَنْ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ أَوْ يَأْتِيَ أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ** ("Do they look for anything but that the angels should come to them, or that your Lord’s edict should come?"), the context of the verse, especially what is in the following verse regarding the account of punishment, is apparent in that it is intended as a threat. Thus, what is meant by the coming of the angels is their descent for the punishment of extermination (عذاب الاستئصال), and it applies to verses like His saying: **ما نُنَزِّلُ الْمَلائِكَةَ إِلَّا بِالْحَقِّ وَما كانُوا إِذاً مُنْظَرِينَ** — "We do not send down the angels except with the truth, and then they will not be granted any respite." (15:8). What is meant by the coming of the Lord's command is the commencement of the Hour, the decisive judgment, and the divine vengeance upon them.

As for the claim that what is meant by the coming of the command is what preceded at the beginning of the Surah in His saying: **أَتى أَمْرُ اللهِ** ("Allah’s edict has come"), where we suggested there that it refers to the coming of victory and the triumph of Islam over polytheism — this does not harmonize well with the severe tone in this verse. Also, there will follow at the end of the verses a mention of their denial of the Resurrection, their insistence on its negation, and the rebuttal against them, which supports the view that what is meant by the coming of the command is the commencement of the Hour.

The Lord added the title to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), saying: **أَمْرُ رَبِّكَ** ("your Lord’s edict") and did not say "Allah's command" or "their Lord's command," to indicate thereby that in it is a victory for him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and a judgment for him against them.

His saying: **كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** ("Those who were before them acted likewise") is a confirmation of the threat and a support by way of precedent; that is, those before them did the like of their deed of rejection and mockery, which naturally entails waiting for Allah's punishment. **فَأَصابهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا** ("So the evils of what they had done overtook them") etc.

His saying: **وَما ظَلَمَهُمُ اللهُ وَلكِنْ كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** ("Allah did not wrong them, but they used to wrong themselves") is a parenthetical statement explaining that the punishment that descended upon them was only necessitated by wrongdoing (الظلم). However, this wrongdoing was their wronging of themselves, not a wrongdoing from Him, the Exalted and Sanctified. Allah, the Glorified, did not punish them for a wrongdoing that occurred from them once or twice; rather, He gave them respite when they wronged until they continued in their wrongdoing and persisted in it — as indicated by His saying: **كانُوا أَنْفُسَهُمْ يَظْلِمُونَ** ("they *used to* wrong themselves"). At that point, He sent down the punishment upon them. Thus, in His saying: **وَما ظَلَمَهُمُ اللهُ** etc., is an affirmation of their persistence in wrongdoing and a negation of the essence of wrongdoing from Allah, the Glorified.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **فَأَصابَهُمْ سَيِّئاتُ ما عَمِلُوا وَحاقَ بِهِمْ ما كانُوا بِهِ يَسْتَهْزِؤُنَ** — "So the evils of what they had done overtook them, and they were besieged by what they used to mock." (16:34). **حاقَ بِهِمْ** means it befell them; it is said its meaning is it descended upon them and struck them. That which they used to mock is the punishment with which their messengers used to warn them. The meaning of the verse is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "The polytheists say, ‘Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers—nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him.’" (16:35) etc. What the verse presents is a doubt (شبهة) against Prophethood from the idolaters who deny it. For this reason, He identified them by their explicit description, saying: **وَقالَ الَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا** ("The polytheists say") and did not suffice with a pronoun or say "and they said" as in the previous verses, so that it might be known that the doubt belongs to them specifically.

His saying: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا** ("Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped") is a conditional sentence in which the object of "wished" (**شاءَ**) is omitted because the apodosis (الجزاء) indicates it. The implied meaning is: "Had Allah wished that we not worship anything besides Him, we would not have worshipped..." etc.

The saying of some — that will (الإرادة) and wish (المشية) do not relate to non-existence (العدم) but only to existence (الوجود), so there is no meaning to a wish for the non-existence of worship; thus, it is better to imply the object of the wish as an existential matter necessitated by the non-existence of worship, such as monotheism (التوحيد) for instance, making the implication: "Had Allah wished that we unify Him or that we worship Him alone, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him" — and he argued using the saying of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **ما شاء الله كان وما لم يشأ لم يكن** ("What Allah wishes, is; and what He does not wish, is not"), where he connected non-existence of being to the absence of a wish, not to a wish for non-existence.

In this is the fact that what he mentioned is true regarding the reality of the matter. However, linguistic focuses and rhetorical expansions do not always revolve around cosmic realities and philosophical views. Simple understandings — and the understandings of those idolaters were no more advanced than that — just as they allow the sequence of an existential act upon a wish, they allow the relation of its non-existence to it. In the speech of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), following this outward focus, it is said: **اللهم إن شئت أن لا تعبد لم تعبد** ("O Allah, if You wish not to be worshipped, You will not be worshipped").

Furthermore, they are alluding by their saying: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا** ("Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped") etc., to the saying of the messengers to them: "Do not associate partners with Allah, do not worship other than Allah, and do not forbid what Allah has made lawful." These are prohibitions, and the meaning of a prohibition is the demand for abandonment (الترك).

Moreover, the idolaters do not deny His oneness, the Exalted, in Divinity (الألوهية) in the sense of making and originating (الصنع والإيجاد). Rather, they associate partners in worship (العبادة), in the sense that they reserve for Him, the Exalted, making and originating, and they reserve for their gods worship. Thus, they have many gods, one of whom is an originating God who is not worshipped, and that is Allah, the Glorified; the rest are worshipped intercessors who do not originate. So they do not worship Allah at all, not that they worship Him, the Exalted, and their gods all together. In that case, if the implication were "Had Allah wished that we unify Him in worship or that we worship Him alone," it would have been more important for the apodosis to be their unifying Him in worship or their worshipping Him alone, not the negation of their worshipping others, or the negation of worshipping others would be an allusion to the unification of His worship or worshipping Him alone. So understand that.

If the object of the wish must be an existential matter, let the implication be: "Had Allah wished that we refrain from worshipping others, we would not have worshipped..." etc., so that the condition and the apodosis unite in reality while they differ in negation and affirmation.

His saying: **ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** ("we would not have worshipped anything besides Him") — the first *min* (مِنْ) is explanatory and the second is redundant to emphasize the exhaustiveness of the negation. The meaning is: we would not have worshipped anything other than Him. Similar to that is His saying: **وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** ("nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him").

His saying: **نَحْنُ وَلا آباؤُنا** ("neither we nor our fathers") is an explanation of the first-person plural pronoun in **عَبَدْنا** ("we worshipped") to indicate that they are speaking for themselves and for all their fathers, because they used to follow their fathers in the worship of idols. The Quran has repeatedly narrated the like of their saying: **إِنَّا وَجَدْنا آباءَنا عَلى أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلى آثارِهِمْ مُقْتَدُونَ** — "‘We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided by their footsteps.’" (43:23).

His saying: **وَلا حَرَّمْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** ("nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him") is conjoined to His saying: **عَبَدْنا** ("we worshipped") etc., meaning: "and had Allah wished that we not forbid anything besides Him, or that we make lawful what we forbade, we would not have forbidden..." etc. What is meant are the *bahirah*, the *sa'ibah*, and others among what they forbade.

Furthermore, their saying: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** etc., is apparent from the perspective of connecting the negation of worship to His very wish, the Exalted, in that they intended by "wish" His creative will (إرادته التكوينية) — *the divine will that is always and necessarily realized in the external world* — which never fails to realize the intended object. Had they intended other than that, they would have said: "Had Allah wished such-and-such, we would have obeyed Him and responded to His call," or what conveys this meaning.

It is as if they are saying: "If the Message were true, and if what the messengers brought of the prohibition against worshipping idols and images, and the prohibition against forbidding the *bahirah*, the *sa'ibah*, the *wasilah*, and others, were prohibitions from Allah, the Glorified, then Allah, the Glorified, would have wished that we not worship anything other than Him and that we not forbid anything besides Him. And had Allah, the Glorified, wished that we not worship others and not forbid anything, we would not have worshipped and we would not have forbidden, because it is impossible for His intended object to fail His will. But we do worship others and we do forbid things; therefore, He does not wish any of that. Thus, there is no prohibition and no command from Him, the Exalted, and no law and no message from His side."

This is the presentation of their argument according to what the context provides. The core of their intent is that their worship of other than Allah and their forbidding of what they forbade — and in short, the generality of their deeds — were not related to a wish from Allah by way of prohibition; for had it been so related, they would necessarily not have done them.

They do not mean by this that Allah's wish related to their worship and their forbidding such that they became necessary in existence and they were compelled (ملجئون) in doing them and forced (مجبرون) in performing them, such that there would be no meaning to the messengers' prohibition after the compulsion. This is because **لَوْ** ("had") indicates the impossibility of the apodosis due to the impossibility of the condition. Thus, the concept of the conditional **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** ("Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him") is that He did not wish that, so we worshipped others. Or if you like, you could say: "But we worshipped others, so it was uncovered that He did not wish that." As for something like our saying: "But He wished that we worship others, so we worshipped others," or our saying: "But He wished that we not unify Him, so we worshipped others," this is foreign to both the concept and the literal wording of the conditional.

Furthermore, had they meant that, and had their purpose been to refute Prophethood by establishing compulsion (الإلجاء) in their actions through the argument they established, they would have been admitting to misguidance (الضلال) and submitting to it, except that they would be excusing themselves from following the guidance brought to them by the messengers by way of compulsion and force — that Allah wished for them the misguidance and misery they are upon of worshipping other than Allah and forbidding what Allah made lawful, and forced them into that, so they are not able to leave it nor capable of deviating from it.

But they are claimants of being rightly guided, persisting in this claim and explicitly stating it, as Allah, the Glorified, narrated from them after mentioning their worship of the angels, when He said: **وَقالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ** — "They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ They have no knowledge of that: they do nothing but conjecture." (43:20) until He said: **بَلْ قالُوا إِنَّا وَجَدْنا آباءَنا عَلى أُمَّةٍ وَإِنَّا عَلى آثارِهِمْ مُهْتَدُونَ** — "Rather they say, ‘We found our fathers following a creed, and we are indeed guided by their footsteps.’" (43:22). It has been repeated in His speech the narration of their justifying the worship of idols as being a national tradition (سنة قومية) sanctified by their predecessors before their successors; thus, it is obligatory for their successors to sanctify it and follow it after their predecessors. Where is this from the admission of misguidance and misery?

Similarly, they do not mean by this argument that their deeds are created by themselves, unrelated to the divine wish, nor that He is their creator. For deeds and actions, on this assumption, would be far removed from the divine will relating to them. Rather, He, the Exalted, would cause the non-performance of an action by creating an obstacle to it. In that case, it would have been more appropriate for them to say: "Had Allah wished, He would have diverted us from worshipping others and forbidding what we forbade," which is refuted by the literal speech. Or they would say: "Had Allah wished anything of our deeds, it would have been nullified and ceased to be a deed of ours while we are independent in it."

Furthermore, if the meaning of their saying "Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped" were that "had He wished, He would have diverted us," it would have been true. In that case, there would be no meaning to His saying, the Exalted, in the previous verse of *al-Zukhruf*: **وَقالُوا لَوْ شاءَ الرَّحْمنُ ما عَبَدْناهُمْ ما لَهُمْ بِذلِكَ مِنْ عِلْمٍ إِنْ هُمْ إِلَّا يَخْرُصُونَ** — "They say, ‘Had the All-beneficent wished, we would not have worshipped them.’ They have no knowledge of that: they do nothing but conjecture." (43:20).

The truth is that they intended by their saying: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** ("Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him") to argue by their worship of them that the divine wish did not relate to abandoning them, without addressing the relation of the wish to the act of worship or the wish being impossible to relate to their worship except by diversion.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَهَلْ عَلَى الرُّسُلِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ** — "Those who were before them acted likewise. Does the Messenger have any duty except a clear delivery [of the message]?" (16:35). This is an address to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) commanding him to deliver his message with a clear delivery and not to pay heed to the argument they fabricated, for it is refuted, and the proof is complete against them by the delivery. In it is a summary indication of the refutation of their argument.

His saying: **كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** ("Those who were before them acted likewise") means: upon this path followed by these people, those before them also walked. They worshipped other than Allah and forbade what Allah did not forbid. Then, when their messengers came to them forbidding them from that, they said: "Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him—neither we nor our fathers—nor would we have forbidden anything besides Him." Thus, the sentence is like His saying, the Exalted: **كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَالَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ كَفَرُوا بِآياتِ اللهِ فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ** — "Like the custom of Pharaoh’s clan and those who were before them, they defied Allah’s signs; so Allah seized them for their sins." (8:52).

His saying: **فَهَلْ عَلَى الرُّسُلِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ** ("Does the Messenger have any duty except a clear delivery?") means: deliver the message to them with a clear delivery by which the proof is completed against them. For the duty of the messengers is only the clear delivery (البلاغ المبين); it is not their duty to compel people to what they call them to and forbid them from, nor to carry with them the necessitating will of Allah that never fails to realize the intended object, nor His command which, when He intends a thing, He says to it "Be" and it is, such that they would thereby transform disbelief into faith and force the sinner into obedience.

For the Messenger is but a human being like them, and the message with which he was sent is a warning and a glad tiding. It is a collection of social laws revealed to him by Allah, in which is the welfare of people in their world and their Hereafter. Its form is that of masterly commands and prohibitions, and its reality is warning and glad tidings. The Exalted said: **قُلْ لا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ عِنْدِي خَزائِنُ اللهِ وَلا أَعْلَمُ الْغَيْبَ وَلا أَقُولُ لَكُمْ إِنِّي مَلَكٌ** — "Say, ‘I do not say to you that I possess the treasuries of Allah, nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I say to you that I am an angel.’" (6:50). This is what He commanded His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to deliver to them, and He had commanded Noah and the messengers after him (عليه السلام) to deliver it to their nations, as in Surah Hud and others.

He also said, addressing His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): **قُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا بَشَرٌ مِثْلُكُمْ يُوحى إِلَيَّ أَنَّما إِلهُكُمْ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ فَمَنْ كانَ يَرْجُوا لِقاءَ رَبِّهِ فَلْيَعْمَلْ عَمَلاً صالِحاً وَلا يُشْرِكْ بِعِبادَةِ رَبِّهِ أَحَداً** — "Say, ‘I am only a mortal like you. It has been revealed to me that your God is the One God. So whoever expects to encounter his Lord—let him act righteously, and not associate anyone with the worship of his Lord.’" (18:110).

This is what He alludes to in a summary fashion by His saying: **كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ فَهَلْ عَلَى الرُّسُلِ إِلَّا الْبَلاغُ الْمُبِينُ**. For its appearance — as we indicated previously — is that this is an argument circulating among them anciently and recently. Accordingly, it is not the business of the Messenger to force people and compel them toward faith and obedience, but rather the clear delivery through warning and glad tidings. Their argument does not refute that. So deliver what you were sent with with a clear delivery, and do not crave the guidance of those among them who have gone astray. The following two verses will detail what this verse summarized and clarify it.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ بَعَثْنا فِي كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولاً أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاجْتَنِبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ هَدَى اللهُ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ الضَّلالَةُ** — "Certainly We raised an apostle in every nation [to preach:] ‘Worship Allah, and keep away from the Rebel.’ Then among them were some whom Allah guided, and among them were some who deserved to be in error..." (16:36) etc. The Rebel (الطاغوت) — *the one who transgresses all bounds of servitude to Allah* — is originally a verbal noun like "transgression" (الطغيان), which is exceeding the limit without right. Its noun form is *al-tughwa* (الطغوى). Al-Raghib said: "*al-Taghut* is an expression for every transgressor and every deity worshipped besides Allah. It is used for both singular and plural. The Exalted said: **فَمَنْ يَكْفُرْ بِالطَّاغُوتِ** ('So one who disbelieves in the Rebel' [2:256]), **وَاجْتَنِبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ** ('and keep away from the Rebel'), and **أَوْلِياؤُهُمُ الطَّاغُوتُ** ('their guardians are the Rebels' [2:257])." [End of quote].

His saying: **وَلَقَدْ بَعَثْنا فِي كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولاً** ("Certainly We raised an apostle in every nation") is an indication that the raising of a messenger is not a matter exclusive to one nation over another. Rather, it is a divine sunnah (سنة إلهية) — *a consistent and established way of Allah's action* — current among all people, insofar as they are in need of it, and it reaches them wherever they may be, as He alluded to its generality in the previous verse summarily by His saying: **كَذلِكَ فَعَلَ الَّذِينَ مِنْ قَبْلِهِمْ** ("Those who were before them acted likewise").

His saying: **أَنِ اعْبُدُوا اللهَ وَاجْتَنِبُوا الطَّاغُوتَ** ("[to preach:] ‘Worship Allah, and keep away from the Rebel’") is an explanation of the raising of the messenger as the context provides. That is, the reality of raising the messenger was nothing other than to call them to the worship of Allah and the avoidance of the Rebel. For a command, as well as a prohibition, from a human being — especially if he is a messenger — is nothing but an ordinary call, not a creative compulsion or necessity. Nor is it for the messenger to claim that, such that it could be refuted by saying: "Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him; and since He did not wish it, there is no meaning to the Message."

From here it becomes apparent that the saying of some — that the implication is "so that he may say to them: worship Allah and avoid the Rebel" — is out of place.

His saying: **فَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ هَدَى اللهُ وَمِنْهُمْ مَنْ حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ الضَّلالَةُ** ("Then among them were some whom Allah guided, and among them were some who deserved to be in error") means: each of these nations was like this nation, divided into two groups. Some of them were those whom Allah guided to what the messenger called them to of worshipping Allah and avoiding the Rebel.

This is because guidance (الهداية) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — is from Allah, the Glorified; no one shares it with Him, and it is not attributed to anyone besides Him except subordinately. As He said: **إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ** — "You cannot guide whomever you wish, but Allah guides whomever He wishes." (28:56). We will allude to this in the following verse: **إِنْ تَحْرِصْ عَلى هُداهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُ** ("Even if you be eager for their guidance, Allah does not guide those whom He leads astray" [16:37]). The verses restricting guidance to Him, the Exalted, are numerous. This does not necessitate it being a matter of compulsion in which the servant has no part at all; for it is voluntary through its preliminaries, as indicated by His saying: **وَالَّذِينَ جاهَدُوا فِينا لَنَهْدِيَنَّهُمْ سُبُلَنا وَإِنَّ اللهَ لَمَعَ الْمُحْسِنِينَ** — "As for those who strive in Us, We shall surely guide them in Our ways, and Allah is indeed with the virtuous." (29:69). This implies that for divine guidance, there is a path made easy for man, which is virtue (الإحسان) in action, and that Allah is with the virtuous, not leaving them to go astray.

And some of these nations — the second group among them — were those who deserved to be in error (حَقَّتْ عَلَيْهِ الضَّلالَةُ), meaning it became established and necessary for them. This error (الضلالة) is that which is from the side of the servant through his evil choice, and it is not that which follows as a requital from Allah. For Allah describes it by saying **حَقَّتْ** ("deserved/became established"), then He attributes it in the following verse to Himself when He says: **فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُ** ("for Allah does not guide those whom He leads astray"). Thus, there was an error, then it became established and fixed by Allah's establishing it as a requital; so it became that which is from Allah, the Glorified, as a requital. So observe carefully.

Allah, the Glorified, has not attributed misguidance (إضلالاً) to Himself in His speech except that which was preceded by wrongdoing (ظلم) from the servant, or transgression (فسق), or disbelief and denial, or their likes. Such as His saying: **وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ** — "and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot." (62:5), and the absence of guidance is misguidance. And His saying: **وَيُضِلُّ اللهُ الظَّالِمِينَ وَيَفْعَلُ اللهُ ما يَشاءُ** — "and Allah leads the wrongdoers astray, and Allah does whatever He wishes." (14:27). And His saying: **وَما يُضِلُّ بِهِ إِلَّا الْفاسِقِينَ** — "and He misleads by it only the transgressors." (2:26). And His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَظَلَمُوا لَمْ يَكُنِ اللهُ لِيَغْفِرَ لَهُمْ وَلا لِيَهْدِيَهُمْ طَرِيقاً إِلَّا طَرِيقَ جَهَنَّمَ** — "Indeed those who are faithless and do wrong, Allah will never forgive them, nor will He guide them to any way, except the way to hell." (4:168-169). And His saying: **فَلَمَّا زاغُوا أَزاغَ اللهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ** — "So when they deviated, Allah made their hearts deviate." (61:5), among other verses.

He, the Glorified, did not say: "among them were some whom Allah guided and among them were some whom He led astray," despite their error being an error of requital which there is no obstacle to attributing to Him, the Exalted. Rather, He mentioned first those He guided, then He contrasted them with those for whom it was right that they be in error — who are those who chose error over guidance, i.e., chose not to be guided — so Allah did not guide them, and that was their right.

To clarify it with another explanation: the summary of the difference between primary error (الضلال الابتدائي) and its attribution to the servant, and error as requital (الضلال مجازاة) and its attribution to Him, the Exalted, and the attribution of guidance — both primary and as requital — to Allah, the Glorified, is that Allah deposited in man the possibility of rectitude and the potential for being guided. If he follows the soundness of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — and does not nullify the potential by following passion and disobedience, or rectifies it through remorse and repentance after disobedience, Allah guides him. This is a guidance of requital from Allah, the Glorified, after the first primordial guidance.

But if he follows his passion and disobeys his Lord, his potential for being guided is nullified, so guidance is not poured upon him; this is his error through his evil choice. If he does not feel remorse and does not return, Allah establishes him in his state, and error becomes his desert (حقت عليه الضلالة); this is error as requital.

Someone might mistakenly imagine that possibility (الإمكان) and potentiality (الاستعداد) must always be two-sided; thus, he who is capable of guidance is also capable of misguidance, and man remains perpetually wavering between existential effects (آثار وجودية) and affirmative acts (أفعال مثبتة), all of which originate from the Exalted, even the initial potentiality and possibility.

This is among the weakest of delusions. To consider the possibility of misguidance and the consequences thereof as an existential matter (أمراً وجودياً) and a Divine bestowal (عطاءً ربانياً) would corrupt the meaning of misguidance and nullify it. Misguidance is only misguidance because it is the absence of guidance (عدم الهداية). If it were to become an affirmative matter (أمراً ثبوتياً), it would not be misguidance; rather, guidance and misguidance would both become existential matters and Divine bestowals, similar to the existential effects resulting from inanimate objects, for instance, which fall outside the scope of guidance and misguidance.

In other words: misguidance is only misguidance when measured against guidance, and it is then necessary that it be the absence of guidance. If it were taken as an existential matter, it would not be misguidance, and the subject would not be divided into the guided and the misguided, nor would its state be divided into guidance and misguidance. Thus, there is no escape from taking misguidance as a privative matter (أمراً عدمياً), and attributing the primary misguidance to the soul of the servant himself. Reflect well upon this, so that your foot does not slip after it has been firm.

His saying: **فَسِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُكَذِّبِينَ** — "Travel through the land and observe how was the fate of the impugners." (16:36). The apparent context indicates that the address is to those who associated partners with Allah, those who said: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him." (16:35). The shift (الالتفات) to addressing them directly is because it is more effective in establishing the argument and completing the proof (إتمام الحجة).

The discourse branches out from what was explained in response to their argument, both generally and in detail. The summary of the meaning is that the Message (الرسالة) and the prophetic call (الدعوة النبوية) do not belong to the creative will (الإرادة التكوينية) — *the irresistible Divine will that brings things into existence* — which would compel the abandonment of idol worship and the prohibition of what Allah has not forbidden, such that they could use the absence of compulsion as evidence for the non-existence of the Message and the falsehood of its claimant. Rather, it is a conventional call (دعوة عادية) through which Allah, the Glorified, sent messengers to call you to the worship of Allah and the avoidance of the Rebel (الطاغوت) — *any power that transgresses the limits of Allah and is worshipped besides Him*. Its reality is warning (الإنذار) and giving glad tidings (التبشير). Evidence for this is found in the remnants of past unjust nations, which recount the descent of punishment upon them: "Travel through the land and observe how was the fate of the impugners," so that it may become clear to you that the prophetic call, which is a warning, is truth, and that the Message is not as you claim.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **إِنْ تَحْرِصْ عَلى هُداهُمْ فَإِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ يُضِلُّ وَما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ** — "Even if you be eager for their guidance, Allah does not guide those whom He leads astray, and they have no helpers." (16:37). Since He explained that past nations were divided into two groups, and one of the two groups were those for whom misguidance was due (حقت عليهم الضلالة), and since these polytheists who said what they said were like those before them, He clarified in this verse that the establishment of misguidance in their regard is an establishment that knows no removal, and a necessity (تحتم) that accepts no change. For in reality, there is no guide except Allah; if their guidance were possible, Allah would be their guide, but He does not guide them because He leads them astray, and guidance and misguidance do not coexist. Furthermore, there is no helper to assist them against Allah so as to compel Him to guide them. Therefore, one should despair of them.

The verse contains a consolation for the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم and a direction for him not to be eager for their guidance, informing him that the decree (القضاء) has already passed regarding them, and "the word is not changed with Me, and I am not unjust to the servants."

So His saying: **إِنْ تَحْرِصْ عَلى هُداهُمْ** — "Even if you be eager for their guidance," etc., is effectively: "If you are eager for their guidance, your eagerness will not benefit them at all, for they are not among those for whom guidance is possible. For Allah is the One who guides whoever is guided, and He does not guide them because He leads them astray, and the Exalted does not contradict His own act, nor do they have helpers to assist them against Him."

In these three verses, there are long disputes between the proponents of compulsion (المجبرة) and the proponents of absolute delegation (المفوضة), each interpreting them according to the requirements of his school of thought. Imam al-Razi even said: "The polytheists intended by their saying: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — 'Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him,' etc., that since everything — monotheism (التوحيد), polytheism (الشرك), guidance (الهدى), and misguidance (الضلال) — is from Allah, the sending of prophets was futile. We say: This is an objection against Allah and is akin to seeking a cause (العلة) for His rulings and acts, and that is invalid. It is not said to Him: 'Why did You do this?' or 'Why did You not do that?'"

He said: "Thus it is established that Allah, the Exalted, only censured these speakers because they believed that the matter being so prevents the permissibility of sending messengers, not because they lied in their statement." (End of summary).

Al-Zamakhshari said: "The polytheists committed the foul deeds they committed and then attributed them to their Lord, saying: 'Had Allah wished...' to the end. This is exactly the doctrine of the proponents of compulsion (المجبرة), just as their predecessors did. Is there anything upon the messengers except to convey the truth? And that Allah does not wish for polytheism and sins through clarification and proof; and that they should realize the falsehood and foulness of polytheism, and the innocence of Allah from the acts of the servants, and that they are the doers of them by their own intent, will, and choice; and that Allah prompts them toward the beautiful among them, granting them success (التوفيق) for it, and restrains them from the foul among them, warning them against it." (End of the relevant portion). They have prolonged the discussion on this from both sides.

You have already realized that the verses aim for a purpose beyond that, and that their meaning in saying: **لَوْ شاءَ اللهُ ما عَبَدْنا مِنْ دُونِهِ مِنْ شَيْءٍ** — "Had Allah wished, we would not have worshipped anything besides Him," etc., was to nullify the Message. Their argument was that if what the messengers brought — the prohibition of worshipping other than Allah and the prohibition of what Allah had not forbidden — were true, then Allah would have willed their abandonment of worshipping others and their prohibition of what He had not forbidden; and if He had willed that, it would have been realized from them, but it was not so. As for whether the Divine will (الإرادة الإلهية) attached to their act such that it became necessary, or whether it did not attach and it is impossible for it to attach, and that their acts are nothing but created by themselves without Allah, the Glorified, having any hand in them — that is a matter outside the implication of their speech and foreign to the argument they established, as derived from the context previously mentioned.

In His saying: **وَما لَهُمْ مِنْ ناصِرِينَ** — "and they have no helpers," there is an indication that others have many helpers. This is because the context indicates they have no helper at all, neither one nor many; thus, negating "helpers" (ناصِرِينَ) in the plural form reveals an extra attention to this point — meaning that there are indeed helpers, but they are not for them, but for others, and those are none other than those who are guided by the guidance of Allah. Similar to this verse is what Allah, the Glorified, recounts from the criminals on the Day of Resurrection: **فَما لَنا مِنْ شافِعِينَ** — "So we have no intercessors." (26:100).

These helpers are the noble angels and all other means of success (التوفيق) and guidance (الهداية), and Allah, the Glorified, encompasses them from behind. The Exalted said: **إِنَّا لَنَنْصُرُ رُسُلَنا وَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا فِي الْحَياةِ الدُّنْيا وَيَوْمَ يَقُومُ الْأَشْهادُ** — "Indeed We shall help Our apostles and those who have faith in the life of the world and on the day when the witnesses rise up." (40:51).

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ لا يَبْعَثُ اللهُ مَنْ يَمُوتُ بَلى** — "They swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths: ‘Allah will not resurrect those who die.’ Yes..." (16:38) to the end of the verse. It is said in *al-Mufradat*: *al-Jahd* (الجهد) — with the *fathah* or *dammah* on the *jim* — means power (الطاقة) and hardship (المشقة), and it is more emphatic than *al-Jahd* with the *fathah*. He said: And the Exalted said: **وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ** — meaning they swore and exerted themselves in the oath to bring it forth in the most emphatic way within their power. (End).

It is said in *al-Majma'* regarding the meaning of His saying: **وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ** — meaning they reached the utmost limit in swearing. (End).

Their saying: **لا يَبْعَثُ اللهُ مَنْ يَمُوتُ** — "Allah will not resurrect those who die," is a denial of the Gathering (الحشر) — *the resurrection and assembly of all beings for judgment*. The sentence is an allusion to the idea that death is annihilation (فناء), so no new creation attaches to it thereafter. This does not contradict the belief of all or most of them in reincarnation (التناسخ), for that is a belief in the soul's attachment, after departing the body, to another body — human or otherwise — and its living in this world; this is what they call "birth after birth" (التولد بعد التولد).

His saying: **بَلى وَعْداً عَلَيْهِ حَقًّا** — "Yes! [It is] a promise binding upon Him in truth," (16:38) meaning the matter is not as they say; rather, Allah will resurrect those who die, a promise He has made a firm and true obligation upon Himself.
